channel.title: Economics & Entrepreneurship on Yi Yao Tan
channel.description: Recent content in Economics & Entrepreneurship on Yi Yao Tan

item.title: Invariants
item.description:
Semantic (Mathematical) definition

An invariant is a feature, quantity, or descriptor of an object that stays unchanged when the object is transformed by the equivalence notion we care about (for example: isometry, linear change of coordinates, isomorphism, homotopy equivalence).

In practice: invariants capture what is intrinsic to the object, independent of representation.

Invariants in Startups/ Technology

Why you should care about invariants

Any space can be divided into a set and its complement as a partition. I believe that the generic information we take in can somewhat be separated into invariants and non-invariants. I believe invariants are like first principles, the minimal set of things we know to be true: a high-signal true-positive space without the need for context (least common positive denominator). Using invariants, we can figure out directions for contextual decision-making and make higher-fidelity, faster decisions, as they generalize better by their sheer nature. This discernment is especially useful in times of volatility and high uncertainty, both geopolitically and technologically.

item.title: First Principles Maths
item.description:
Some of my theories and formalizations of what linguistic computing from business people tries to approximate. Notes here were jotted down as unorganized thoughts, so bear with me.

  1. Doing things which don’t scale/ Good startup ideas: are startup ideas which have high uncertainty for others; however, they have low uncertainty but a high probability of success for founders based on their knowledge. Not high uncertainty and low probability, because that’s just absolutely unlikely. Innovation actually happens in fractal branching processes, where a vibe-coding frontend can emerge from combining the known need of website builders with control of technical infrastructure for future scale.

item.title: B2b
item.description:
B2B Mathematics of why it’s so sexy.

Sometimes people wonder if over investment of B2B is a bubble. Like why do people love it, why does Y Combinator focus on it. Why do all entreprises over the long term converge to it. I have four theories:

  1. Locality of information at entreprise scale, to build something and a fitting product for an entreprise is really hard and even to discover a tool which is perfect for the entreprise is really hard, communication is like square cube law if square was the surface area of the size of the entreprise, to route something to an entreprise which fits it will scale even harder, which signifies the importances of sales and stickiness of the product.

item.title: Topology of startups
item.description:
1. The Topological View of the World

  • We exist socially, experience-wise, and physically on a topology
  • We operate in cognitive bias where opinions are actually strong local views.
  • Understanding locality bias and thinking in distances helps us view things in perspectives

2. The “AI Bubble” Phenomenon

  • Big tech and AI employees actually self selected, socially collaborative filtered for their niche.

  • The things the say about AGI and ASI are from the sum of their experiences. In a sense the ultimateness comes from their experiences so far. Software engineering is gone etc. etc. (which is conditioned on their notion of the “Software Engineering that they experienced has changed” phrasing on what is concrete and truly (real) sense)
